Circular library stamp of the Advocatorum Bibliotheca.A circular library stamp is centered on a dark brown, textured book cover. The stamp is composed of a laurel wreath surrounding a central sunburst emblem. The text "ADVOCATORUM" is arched across the top of the wreath, and "BIBLIOTHECA" is arched across the bottom. The entire stamp is embossed into the leather.

A blank, aged, cream-colored page with visible texture and minor blemishes.This image shows a blank, aged, cream-colored page, likely an endpaper or flyleaf from an old book. The paper has a subtle, fine-grained texture and shows signs of wear, including several small, faint brown spots (foxing) and a few larger, more distinct stains, particularly along the right edge and bottom. The overall color is a warm, off-white or light beige.

PRICE LIST

page 11 · 192890211.3.jpg

GENERAL INDEX.

THIS Index is intended not merely to facilitate reference to the Articles and Treatises contained in the ENCYCLOPÆDIA, but to serve the far more important purpose of bringing into view, and directing the inquirer to the varied information scattered through them, but not indicated by their heads or titles—a requisite which the plan of general treatises and systems adopted in this ENCYCLOPÆDIA renders particularly desirable. In this way much information upon various topics not so indicated will be found in these Treatises; and it is obviously of great importance, that those making inquiries regarding such topics, should be enabled to turn at once to the places in which that information may be found. For instance, a reader desirous of learning as much as possible about Scotland and Scottish affairs, will not only find, under the head SCOTLAND, a general view of the history and statistics of the country, but be referred by the Index to many other parts of the work (such as BRITAIN, FISHERIES, ROADS, &c.), where a great variety of additional particulars are furnished. Again, as the sciences are discussed in the ENCYCLOPÆDIA under their general names, the principal details embraced by them are given under such heads as ANATOMY, CHEMISTRY, ENTOMOLOGY, METAPHYSICS, METEOROLOGY, &c. By consulting the Index, therefore, the student will be saved the trouble of looking through a long treatise in search of information upon particular points; and those, again, who are in quest of information which they might not think was to be found under such heads, may, in the same way, be enabled unexpectedly to extend their knowledge. Those indexes which have already been given at the end of the Preliminary Dissertations, and of the articles on the natural history of animals and some other sciences, are here embodied under one Alphabet.

It is necessary to observe, that, with the proper character of an Index, that of a Table of Contents is not here combined. For example, under the head SCOTLAND in the Index, no reference whatever is made to the contents of the article so headed in the ENCYCLOPÆDIA; it being presumed that a person in quest of knowledge about Scotland will commence by turning over the pages of that article, and so ascertaining its contents. But the particulars there given enter the Index under the various heads of AGRICULTURE, BANKS, COAL, FISHERIES, IRON, LIBRARIES, RAILROADS, and so forth.

The name of every subject to which an article is devoted in the ENCYCLOPÆDIA, is printed in capital letters in the Index; and the first reference is to the volume and page where the article occurs. The immediately subsequent references are, as generally as possible, arranged according to the fulness of the information to which they point; and hence the probability is increased, that, by turning to the places first indicated, the reader will find the information he desires. On subjects undistinguished by capital letters in the Index, no separate articles occur in the ENCYCLOPÆDIA.

When an article in the Index contains many references to countries and towns, the names of the latter are alphabetically arranged, in the manner exemplified under the heads AGRICULTURE, ARMY, CANALS, &c. The adoption of this plan will save time and trouble to persons consulting the Index. In many chemical articles, such as Oxides, Sulphurets, &c., a similar arrangement is followed. References to maps are always given last.

Many French names have Le, La, De, Des, or Du, prefixed to them; as Le Clerc, La Harpe, La Place, De Moivre, Du Halde, Des Cartes. Sometimes both words are thrown into one; as Laplace, Lagrange. It is

page 12 · 192890224.3.jpg
ii

a question whether, in such cases, the initial letter of the prefix, or that of the distinctive word, ought to determine the position of the name in the Index. Except where both words are usually joined, no general rule has been strictly adhered to in the body of the work; for the most part, however, the prefix has been disregarded—so that Du Halde, for example, appears under the letter H, and Le Clerc under the letter C. In compiling the Index, the following principle has been adopted:—Where a name is commonly printed as two words (Le Clerc, for instance), the latter determines its place in the Index; while in such cases as Laplace, Descartes, &c., the former is followed. It may be well, however, for the reader to look at both places when any doubt is entertained.

Many individuals are better known by their titles than by their surnames; thus, Henry Home is currently referred to as Lord Kames, and Sir Thomas Wentworth as the Earl of Strafford. To avoid confusion, it is customary in Biographical Dictionaries to adopt the surname as the word regulating the position of the article; and from this sound practice there is, in the present Index, almost no deviation that the compiler's knowledge enabled him to avoid. As, however, some readers must be presumed to be ignorant of the surnames of men whose titles are their ordinary designations, titles also are generally inserted, with merely a reference to the surnames. To make inquiry secure, both ought to be consulted.

The only additional remark which seems called for is, that in proper names in which the diphthong æ or œ occurs, the diphthong is retained, instead of being supplanted by the letter e, as is now sometimes the case. The reader will therefore turn to Ætna, not Etna; and to Æolus, not Eolus.

R. C.

page 13 · 192890237.3.jpg

ALEMERT (Jean le Rond d'), II. 397—his classification of the sciences incorrect, I. 1—vagueness of his views relative to their origin, lb.—his unsuccessful delineation of an encyclopaedic tree, 3—quotation from, on his division of human knowledge, 3—4—objections to this, 5—5—his definition of poetry 4—his identification of imagination with abstraction, 6—his modesty in presupposing his theory, 7—his encyclopaedic tree only an amplification of Bacon's sketch, 7—8—character of his preliminary discourse on the division of the sciences, 10—quotation from, on the bias of the mind produced by habits acquired in infancy, 64—his mistaken view of the nature of space and time, 142—mathematical researches of, 578—expanded the process of integration relative to partial differences, 603—on dynamics, 694; VIII. 387; XIV. 553—sketch of his life and character, I. 604—converted dynamics into an absolute analytical science, 605—treated the subject of hydrodynamics with his usual originality and depth, 608—on achromatic glasses, II. 104—his connection with Diderot, VIII. 14—on double employment in music, 122—on the resolution of forces, 238—on the earth's figure, IX. 551—on imitative music, XV. 641.

ALGERIA, II. 420—invention of, I. 440; XIV. 315—first introduced into Europe by Leonardo, I. 440; XIV. 316—introduced by Gerbert, I. 441—first book printed on, lb.—system of Diophanthus, 591—resolution of equations higher than the second degree, and extension of the rule by Cardan, lb.—merit of Cardan's discoveries, 442—he gave his algebraic rules a poetical dress, lb.—properties of algebraic equations discovered very slowly, lb.—contributions to the science by Recorde, Bombelli, Vieta, Girard, Harriot, and others, 442-8. See also these heads, and 591-2—canoes why the properties of equations were discovered so slowly, 445—application of algebraic analysis by Descartes, to define the nature and investigate the properties of curve lines, 444—analysis of the work in which that discovery is announced, lb.—advantages resulting from the introduction of algebra into geometry, lb.—denotes both quantity and the operations on quantity by means of conventional symbols, 445—excellence of this, lb.—difference in practice between the algebraic and geometrical method of treating quantity, 445—observations on the use of the signs plus and minus, 446—extraction of the cube root first discovered by the Arabians, 583—observations on the algebraic signs, 595—Descartes's contributions to, 594; VI. 181—proposal of the Conchoid, by Newton, I. 595—advance made in, by the discoveries of Leibniz, lb.—discoveries of De Moivre and Stirling, 595—theory of quadratic factors, 595—account of improvements made in algebra, 596-7—applications of, and notices of elementary works on, lb.—valuable results of the introduction of, 444—of lines and conics, XIV. 323—assumptions treated algebraically, III. 270. See Equations, Negative Sign, Series, &c.

Animals—changes on European animals naturalized in America, II. 655—difficulty of defining the term, III. 155—wherein different from plants, II. 683; III. 155-8—substances of which composed, 158—harmonious combination of their organs, 159—variety of organization among, 160—classification of, lb., 160, et seq.; II. 703; XVII. 549, 585—classifications of Linnaeus, III. 171; Virrey, lb.; Lamarck, 172; Duméril, 177; Hladaville, 177; Macleay, 178; and Cuvier, 179—no change of species produced by time, climate, or other secondary causes, 169—geographical distribution of, XVII. 549; III. 161; II. 652—what constitutes a species, III. 161—hybrids generally barren, lb.—doctrine of equivocal generation of, 162; XXI. 992—brain and nervous system in different classes of, III. 163; see Brain, &c.—insensibility of many to pain, 164—cruelty to, 165—senses of, 165—aliment of, 166; see Food—nature of the flesh of different, lb.—power of resisting starvation, 167; II. 46—hibernation of, III. 167—pairing of, 168—obscure case of animal and vegetable life combined, 166, 167—electricity of, VIII. 669—effects of light on, XIII. 349—effects of electricity on, VIII. 638—effects of galvanism on, XXI. 681—equilibrium and motion of, XIV. 389—strength of, 430—extinct, XV. 275—geological succession of, 238—number of species, XVIII. 549—application of physiology to zoology, 545—imaginary gradation of animals, 557—mutual destruction of, 569, note—small animals remarkable for strength and agility, XX. 770. ARABIANS, or Saracens, of the middle ages—the first discoverers in Africa, II. 220—the invention of algebra claimed by them, 421—their conquest of Algiers, 503—found of astrology, 531—2—anatomy little improved by them, 691—science cultivated by them, 692—their architecture, III. 378—astronomy, 734—numerical digits, II. 7, 12—introduction of these into Europe, III. 359, 540—their chemical knowledge, VI. 342—their dials, VII. 759—were the most enlightened people during the middle ages, VIII. 477—their attention to geography, X. 396—were ignorant of the compass, 527—their knowledge of gunpowder, XI. 76—their conquests, III. 680; XI. 480—their study of mathematics, X. 427; XIV. 515, 516—their art, XVI. 707—their knowledge of anatomy and physiology, XVII. 729; and of surgery, XX. 825. ARISTOYRE, III. 499—causes which operated in undermining the authority of, I. 16—by whom most powerfully assailed, 23—on rhetoric, 54; III. 514; XIX. 289, et seq.—remarks on his comparison of the mind in its first state to a sheet of white paper, I. 364—resemblance of Home's principles of association to the views of, 427—his definition of motion, 450; and of light, 503—his views regarding the nature of light, 649—his anatomical knowledge, II. 657—his astronomy, III. 502, 729—his physical tenets, 499; IV. 354—possibly derived his system of logic from India, V. 189—high estimation of his works in the middle ages, VII. 495—on dew, 748—on the dramatic unities, VIII. 167, 174—his Natural History, III. 506, 508; XII. 151; XIV. 74; XVII. 725—on comedy, XIII. 121—the prince of logicians, 439—on mathematics, III. 479—on mechanics, XIV. 319—on metaphysics, III. 499; XIV. 599—on perception, III. 507; XIV. 604—on matter, 643—on form, 644—on motion, 457—on soul, III. 506; XIV. 636—on tragedy, XV. 446—on optics, XVI. 348—on ornithology, 545—compared with Newton, XVII. 435—on physiognomy, 575—on plastic nature, 797—on the air, XVIII. 71—on quality, 773—on quantity, 775—his Analytics, III. 511; XIX. 166—his Topics, XIX. 166—his Disiectic, III. 508; VII. 756—his Poetics, III. 517—his Ethics, 518—his Politics, 525—design of his philosophy, 429—style of his writings, 539—his obscurity, lb.—his method of discussion, 530—his originality, 531—Socrates a precursor of, XX. 468—his influence on medicine, 822—his opinions in Natural Theology, XXI. 262—says little about the tides, 260.
ARISTOTLE'S PHILOSOPHY, III. 494—Mentheton's sanction of, I. 16.
ARISTOXENUS, III. 531.
ARITHMETIC, III. 532—early systems of notation, I. 586—origin of the decimal system generally referred to India, 567—advantages and gradual extension of this system, lb.—decimal fractions introduced by Stevinus, lb.—notation of decimals brought to its utmost simplicity by Baron Napier, lb.—Advantages and disadvantages of the duodecimal scale, lb.—perfect, prime, and composite numbers, 558—antiquity of magic squares, lb.—different individuals by whom they were constructed, lb.—continued fractions much cultivated during the 18th century, 569—brought to perfection by Euler, lb.—applications of the theory by him, lb.—applied to the solution of numerical equations by Lagrange, 569—tables of the powers and products of numbers made by Hervert, lb.—of the Greeks, Romans, and Arabians, II. 5, 6, 7, 21—proportion in, 455—of sines, 455—alligation, or solution of

questions relating to the mixture of ingredients, 526—binary, IV. 643—Chinese knowledge of, VI. 575—druidical knowledge of, VIII. 207—Napier's rods, XV. 693—arithmetical board for the blind, IV. 698. See Number, Series, Addition, &c.

centres of uniform motion, accom-
modated to the state of science, lb.—
dawn of a new era, Copernicus and
Tycho, 484—correction of Ptolemy's
tables by Alphonso, king of Castile,
lb.—Purbach and Regiomontanus con-
tributed much to the advancement of
the science, lb.—publication of Cop-
ernicus's great work, Astronomia In-
stantanea, containing the discovery of
the earth's annual and diurnal mo-
tion, 485—observations of the heavens
by Tycho Brahe, 486; see Brahe—
discoveries of Kepler; see Kepler—
discoveries of Galileo; see Galileo—
evidence of the Copernican system de-
veloped by the discoveries of Kepler
and Galileo, 492—beneficial results of
its being established, 495—Descartes'
theory of the universe; see Descartes—
first complete system of astronomy in
which the elliptic orbits were in-
troduced was the Astronomia Philo-
sophica of Bullialdus, 495—hypothetical
views contained in that work, lb.—
contributions to the science by Her-
rox, Ward, Riccoll, Herelius, Hook,
Casini, and Roemer; see these heads—
first establishment of academies,
499—figure and magnitude of the
earth; see Figure of the Earth—dis-
covery of universal gravitation by
Newton, 504; see also Newton—refer-
ences of the ancients to weight or
gravity, 557—earlier views of Cop-
ernicus and Kepler, 558—Galileo sup-
posed that gravity was a principle be-
longing to each of the planets indi-
vidually, but did not extend from the
one to the other, lb.—near approxima-
tion to the truth by Hook, 559—
causes of irregularities in the motion
of the moon, 560—Newton's deter-
mination of the shape of the earth, 561
—discovery of the aberration of light
by Bradley, 569—Newton's theory of
the moon, a work of genius almost
superhuman, 563—lunar theory first
expanded and improved by Calandrini,
lb.—completed by Dr Stewart and
Walmsley, 564—examination of the
subject by Clairaut, lb.—astronomical
discoveries of Mayer, 565; see also
Mayer—calculations and discoveries of
Euler regarding the motions of the
planets, 565-6—appearance of Halley's
comet, and calculations of Clairaut
regarding its retardation, 567—size
and consistency of comets, and meth-
ods of discovering their orbits, 568
—notice of Lagrange's discourse on
the libration of the moon, 569—re-
searches of Lagrange and Laplace re-
garding the planetary motions, lb.—
Laplace's discovery of the moon's sec-
ular equation, 570—Laplace's com-
plete theory of the motions of Jupi-
ter's satellites, lb.—description of ce-
lestial refraction, and the solar and
lunar parallax, 571—discovery of a
new planet by Herschel, 572—dis-
covery of four small planets, 573—num-
ber of stars which passed over Her-
schel's field of view in a quarter of an
hour, lb.—speculations regarding the
celestial bodies and the constitution
of the universe, 574—various esti-
mates of the number of comets within
the known extent of our system, 575
—observations on the earth's internal
structure, lb.; on the limits of the at-
mosphere, 576; on double and mul-
tiple stars, 577—Aristotle on, III,
562—catalogues of the stars, VI, 238
—Chinese knowledge of, 578—Indian,
VII, 631—knowledge of the Bruids in,

ASTRONOMY.

ASSIMILATION, III, 716—in physiology,
XVII, 581, 618.

ATMOSPHERES, IV. 132—discovery of its elasticity, I. 481—experiments to ascertain its density at different heights, 613—sound transmitted by the, II. 119, 115—velocity of sound, XX. 482—theory of its navigation, II. 178—diminution of its temperature at increasing heights, 195, 196—its composition the same at great heights as at the earth's surface, 195—its refringent power, III. 762—of the sun, 783—moon, IV. 5—Venus, 28; XVIII. 105—Mars, IV. 29; XVIII. 165—Jupiter, XVIII. 105—and comets, ib.—discovery of its pressure, IV. 366—gradation of its density, 398—G. Beccharin on its electricity, 567—why warmest at its lowest parts, VI. 759—its capacity for heat at different degrees of rarity, 759—circulation of air between the poles and the equator, 758—air lost by water in the act of freezing, VII. 51—quantity of, necessary for respiration, VIII. 59—remarkably dry and saline in Egypt, 514—electricity of the, 614—no muriatic acid in sea-air, IX. 453—resistance of the, XI. 47; XVIII. 644—specific gravity of, XII. 26—relations of air to heat, XII. 114—mutual repulsion of its particles, 129—air emitted by vegetables, 129—magnetic condition of the, XIII. 742, 753—influence of its purity and hygroscopic state on health, XIV. 513—its colour, 740; XVI. 510; XVII. 530—its height, XIV. 719; XVIII. 74, 102—its constitution, XIV. 719—the variations of its local weight unexplained, 721—new theory proposed, 722—how affected by heat and moisture, 721—law of atmospheric solution, 735—quantity of moisture held in the, 736—its extent, XV. 178—moon's influence on the, 442—unusual refraction of the, XVII. 563—effects of respiration on, XVIII. 616—mechanical properties of, XVIII. 69—its necessity to animal life, 121—its density and weight, XVII. 579; composition, 530—aqueous vapour in the, 552—Dito Guericke's experiments on its pressure, XX. 422—effect of its pressure on the level of the sea, XXI. 295—necessary to germination, 567—changes on it thereby produced, 568—food derived by plants from it, 571—necessary to vegetation, 575—changes on it thereby produced, ib.—577, 579, 583, 584—whether on the whole purified or deteriorated by plants, 578—atmospheric air, in mineralogy, XV. 137. BACON, curing of, II. 389; VIII. 282.—(FRANCIS) Baron of Verulam, XXI. 605—his philosophy, XVII. 433, 572—his outline of the various departments of human knowledge imperfect, I. 1—3—objections to his classification of the sciences and arts according to a logical division of our faculties, 5, 6—his attempt to accomplish this, however, productive of great advantages to science, 7—his comprehensiveness of mind, ib.—his opinion of Paracelsus, 18—his genius peculiarly adapted to the study of the phenomena of mind, 32—his definition of poetry, 33—general comprehensiveness of his minds and reflections relative to the philosophy of the mind, and its relation to matter, ib.—on the reciprocal influence of thought and language, 34—profound reflections of, on grammar, ib.—his ethical disquisitions generally of a practical nature, 35—his opinion that the faculties of man have declined as the world has grown older, erroneous, ib.—character of his Essays, 36—on philosophical jurisprudence, ib.—on "deep and vulgar laws," ib.—paramount importance attached by, to the education of the people, 38—character of, by Ben Johnson and others, 230—40—Condorcet's estimate of his powers, 54—his works little read in France till after the publication of D'Alembert's preliminary discourse, ib.—on human reason, 58—his admiration of the Epicurean physics, 71—analysis of his Novum Orga-

page 29 · 192890445.3.jpg
INDEX.
17
BAC—BAD

non, 454, 478, et seq.—low state of physics in the time of, ib.—on the causes of vagueness and sterility in all the physical sciences, ib.—causes of error enumerated under four heads called "Idols," 455-6—does not charge the ancients with being absolutely regardless of experiment, 454—reduces the periods during which science had been cultivated, to that of the Greeks, that of the Romans, and that of the western nations after the revival of letters, ib.—considers the end and object of knowledge to have been very much mistaken, 457—exemplification of the nature of induction, 458—classifies all learning relatively to memory, reason, and imagination, ib.—distribution of knowledge under these heads, 459—explanation of the latent process, and the latent schematism, ib.—method of exemplifying the process of induction relative to the form or cause of any thing, 460—intended that his method should be applied to all investigations where experience is the guide, ib.—all facts not of equal value in the discovery of truth, 461—enumeration of twenty-seven different species of facts, or instances, with examples of these, 461-6—quotation from, on those experiments which most immediately tend to improve art by extending science, 467—remarks on the philosophical instruments then known, ib.—sagacious anticipation of facts regarding light afterwards discovered, quoted, ib.—general estimate of his genius, 468—Hume's comparison between him and Galileo, 469—qualities in which he excelled Galileo, as well as all mankind, 470—views of, regarding the nature of heat, 470—his confused ideas on aeromaths, II, 189—on the nourishment of plants and animals, III, 166—his philosophy compared with Aristotle's, 496-7, 509—treats Aristotle unjustly, 514—on the balance of power, IV, 369, 511—on bodily deformity, VII, 674, 676—on the compressibility of water, XII, 9—on law-reform, XIII, 189—on insanity, XIV, 579—on eloquence, XIX, 212—not free from superstition, XX, 812.
—(JOHN), IV, 276—character of his sculpture, XX, 9.
—(SIR NICHOLAS), IV, 275.
—(ROGER), IV, 275—his claim to the respect of posterity, I, 464—pursued the true philosophy amidst ignorance and error, 597—advancement made by, in optics, ib.; XIII, 329; XVI, 531—probable that he made experiments with lenses, and knew their properties, I, 597—his inclination to the marvelous, 598—on the art of flying, II, 177—ignorant of the denary numerals, III, 541—not the inventor of gunpowder, ib., n. 1; XI, 76—on astronomy, III, 756—his chemical and other knowledge, VI, 343.

BAYLE (Peter), IV. 466; VII. 265—the first writer who led to the misapplication of the term Scandalum, I. 147—character of his writings, 151—opinions of the learned at the time of his appearance divided between Aristotle and Descartes, 152—took advantage of this by keeping aloof from all, and indulging his scepticism, &c.—probable reason for the omission of Montaigne in his Dictionary, &c.—copied the spirit and tone of the old academic school, 153—his character by Warburton, Leibnitz, and Gibbon, &c.—his critical acumen unrivalled, but his portraits of persons defective, 154—causes which contributed to unsettle his opinions, &c.—early fluctuations of his religious creed, &c.—his propensity to treat of indolent subjects, &c.—mischievous tendency of his work, 155—benefits which have resulted to literature from his labours, &c.—his leaning to the system of the Manicheans apparent, but not real, &c.—estimate of his genius and acquirements, 156—lessons of historical scepticism to be learned from, &c.—on anonymous books, IV. 627—his Dictionary, XV. 599.

BEN.—BER

BENOWN, African town, IV. 572.
BENEKRADE (Isaac de), IV. 572.

Benson (Dr), on structure of bones,

— (George), IV. 573.
Bentham (General), on Brunel's block-
machinery, IV. 704—his improve-
ments in dock-yards, VIII. 76, 77, 84,
88.
— (Jeremy), on the law of nature,
I. 32-3—on blind veneration for anti-
quity, 95—character of him and his
followers, 377—his Fragment on Go-
vernment, ib.—his tract upon the
Hard Labour Bill, ib.—his Letters on
Usury, ib.—his writings on Jurispru-
dence, 578; XII. 637, et seq.—has
not reached the most desirable dis-
tinction in Ethical Theory, I. 579—
preaches the doctrine of utility with
the zeal of a discoverer, ib.—confuses
moral approbation with the moral
qualities, ib.—his followers have over-
looked the inward delight which arises
from virtuous conduct, 380—his
merit as a reformer of Jurisprudence,
384—treats Ethics too juridically, ib.
—coincides with the Epicureans in
some points, ib.—revolution which his
style underwent, 385—Dumont's literary
connection with, 386; VIII. 265,
266—on the penal code, XII. 639—on
legislation, XIII. 167, 177, 192—on
prisons, XVIII. 574, 586, et seq.—on
punishment, 731.

BERGOO, African country, IV. 583.

BERKA, Berle, or Berry, IV. 584.
BERING (Simon), Danish poet, IV. 584.
Beris, insect, IX. 264.
BERKELEY (George), bishop of Cloyne,
IV. 584—refutation of a fallacy in his
work on Vision, I. 244—his interview
with Malebranche, 80—character of
his genius, 163—Pope's veneration
for, ib.—his popularity made meta-
physics fashionable, 164—chief aim of
his work on Vision, ib.—his doctrine
unknown to the ancients, ib.—merit
of the new theory of vision not exclu-
sively his own, 165—did not lay claim
to complete originality, 166—sense in
which he employed the term sugges-
tion, 167—his object in denying the
existence of matter, 168—value which
he attached to his system of idealism,
and the impression it made, 168, 169
—his theory of Vision contains a
great discovery in mental philosophy,
549—his speculations not sceptical,
ib.—his talents and virtues praised by
all, 550—his attempt to reclaim the
natives of North America, ib.—made
bishop of Cloyne, ib.—his patriotism
with regard to Ireland, ib.—his gen-
eral principles of Ethics, 551—his
graceful and beautiful diction, ib.;
XXI. 89—his reasonings undoubtedly
produced the scepticism of Hume, I.
552—notice of his Analyst, 554—his
attack on the calculus, IX. 637—his
philosophy, XII. 296—on the analysis
of infinites, XIV. 323—on abstract
ideas, XIV. 622—on the non-existence
of matter, 642—on the source of mo-
tion, XV. 576—on Plato, XVII. 797
—on wealth, XVIII. 273, note 2.
—, in Gloucestershire, IV. 585—
vale of, X. 587—ship canal, XVI. 13
—murder of Edward II. in the castle
of, VIII. 727.
BERKHAMSTEAD (Great), IV. 585.
BERKOYOSHA, Turkish city, IV. 586.
BERKSHIRE, IV. 586—breed of pigs, II.
338—Wilt Canal, XVI. 21.

144—Royal Library of, XIII. 313—
university of, XVIII. 629.
—, vehicle, IV. 587.

BER

BERNARD (the Great St), mountain in

Bernardin (Gunga), on anatomy, II. 762.
BERNARDINE (St), IV. 589.

BERNER, Berneray, or BERNER, an

Bernede, bird, XVI. 688.
BERNER (Francis), French traveller,
IV. 590—his writings, I. 103.
— (Nicholas), musician, IV. 590.
BERNER'S ISLAND, IV. 590.
Bernieres (Monsieur), his large burnings,
V. 728.
BERNIS (John L.), IV. 590—his style
of architecture, III. 427—his sculpture,
XX. 8.
BERNOUILLI (Daniel), IV. 594—on hy-
drodynamics, I. 667; XII. 3, 4, 76—
sketch of his life and character, I.
607—singularly happy in his sober
application of analysis, 668—clearly
established the true theory of the vi-
brations of a musical chord, 612—on
the gradation of atmospheric density,
IV. 398—on the resolution of forces,
VIII. 353—his dipping needle, XIII.
768—on mechanics, XIV. 335—on
probability, XVIII. 592—on the re-
sistance of fluids, XIX. 176—his pro-
posed steam-navigation, XX. 687.
— (JAMES), IV. 591—an able co-
adjutor of Leibnitz in illustrating the
new analysis, I. 520—explained some
of the most difficult problems of the
infinitesimal analysis, 529—works of,
530—his original mode of treating
the problem of the centre of oscilla-
tion, 603—sketch of his life and charac-
ter, ib.—his formula for the
chords of multiple arcs, II. 493—
his contributions to mathematics,
XIV. 319—his dispute with his bro-
ther, 320—on probability, XVIII.
592—his problem of the elastic curve,
XX. 762—on the calculus of varia-
tions, XXI. 557.
— (JAMES), the younger, IV. 595.
— (JOHN), IV. 592—an able coad-
jutor of Leibnitz in illustrating the
new analysis, I. 520—judgment pro-
nounced by him on the controversy
between Newton and Leibnitz, 522—
solution of problems by, 524—by the
introduction of exponential equations
materially improved a branch of the
calculus, 526—works of, 530—was the
first who fully enunciated the proposi-
tion of the equality of the opposite
moments, 535—his prize-essay on the
laws of the communication of mo-
tion, 537—simplified the science of
equilibrium or statics by his prin-
ciple of virtual velocities, 603—his
principle styled the conservation of
living forces, ib.—sketch of his life
and character, ib.—quotation from his
defence of Leibnitz's law of conti-
nuity, 133—had a conviction of this
law previously to communicating with
Leibnitz upon the subject, 238—his
formula for the chords of multiple
arcs, II. 493—his barometer, IV.
391—on the inverse problem of cen-
tripetal bodies, VIII. 386—Kiel's
challenge to, IX. 636—his contribu-
tions to mathematics, XIV. 320, et
seq.—on projections, XVIII. 115, 642
—on measures of force, 780—on the
calculus of variations, XXI. 557.

BRAIN (HUMAN), description of, by GALEN, II. 630, and VAROLINUS, 697—Willis on anatomy of, 639—anatomical researches on, 704—structure of, 736; XVIII. 681—its anatomy, II. 806—weight, lb.—convolutions, 897—ventricles, 898—blood vessels, 811—membranes, lb.—connection with the nutritive organs, 813—its functions, III. 159; XIV. 579, 582; XVIII. 454—its chemical properties and composition, VI. 504; XVII. 650—effect of oxygenated blood on, VIII. 291—connection of the mind with, VIII. 441; XIV. 671; XVII. 454, 659, 674, 681—Gali's opinions concerning, X. 297; XVII. 454—proportionally large in infants, XII. 271—Leuwenhoek's observations on, XIII. 256—is necessary to sensation, XIV. 663—dependence of memory on its state, 617—human compared

BRA

with those of brutes, 185; XVIII. 699, 706, 702—theory of its galvanic functions, 131—effects of injuries and diseases of the, 457, 459, 465—theory of plurality of organs in, 458, 466, 469, 681—how far correspondent in shape with the skull, 469, 465—is the seat of insanity, XIV. 584—its appearance in cases of insanity, XVIII. 124—often diseased, without symptoms of functional derangement, 478, 480—When one hemisphere is diseased may the other perform its functions? 479—organic and dynamical disease of, 481—sympathetic diseases of, 484—softening of, 489—complication of diseases of, 492—What part of it is the sensorium? 673—What part connected with voluntary motion? 675-6. See Phrenology.

det's character of, I. 58—not entitled
to be called the father of experimental
physics, but of the experimental
philosophy of the mind, 67-8—his
clear and precise conception of reflec-
tion, 57—on the mind, ib. et seq., 72
—the articles of common belief which
he proposed to subject to severe scruti-
ny, 59—on consciousness of exis-
tence, ib.; XIV. 631—his argument
for the existence of the Deity, I. 59
unjustly persecuted as an atheist, 80
—his precocity of genius, ib.—princi-
pal articles of his philosophy, ib.—his
claims to the discovery of some lead-
ing ideas ascribed to later metaphysi-
cians, 63—progress of his doctrines in
England, 64—merits of his Meditations,
43—coincidences between passages in
his works and the Novum Organum,
244—his errors, 68—on the connec-
tion between soul and body, ib.—on
extension and figure, 245—on the pri-
mary and secondary qualities of bodies,
63, 245—reasons why he fixed on the
pineal gland as the seat of the soul,
69—never mentions Bacon, 70—er-
roneously called a nullist, 246—his
merits as a writer, 247—his doctrines
concerning ideas misunderstood, 169,
251—on the freedom of the will, 267
—supposition of Coleridge that he ant-
cipated Hobbes in his discourse on
Method erroneous, 428—in philosoph-
izing pursued a course opposite to the
Baconian, 468—comparison of his
system with Bacon's, 469—on the
theory of motion, 478—on the pres-
ervation of the same quantity of motion
in the universe, ib.—on centrifugal
force, ib.—the first who attempted to
reduce all the phenomena of the uni-
verse to the same law, 495—his theory
of vortices, 494—his claim to the dis-
covery of the true law of refraction ill
founded, 511—his theory of light,
512—his optical discoveries, 512-13,
631; XVI. 358, 403—on the force of a
moving body, I. 538—his improvements
in, and new applications of, algebra,
I. 444; II. 424; IX. 632—on the
pressure of the atmosphere, IV. 388
—his barometer, 389—his attempt

289—his opinion of brutes, V. 613—

tions to mathematics, XIV. 317;

XVIII. 74—on force and velocity,
779—his theory of the origin of
springs, XX. 545.

Ethical philosophy, Bacon's disquisitions on, I. 32—Hobbes's principles, 42—Cudworth's system, 44—little

ETH—ETI

attention paid to, at the era of the Restoration, I. 46—Barrow on, lb.—Dr Linn's system, 171-2—Division of the subject, 296-7—Paley's views, 298—retrospect of ancient ethics, 299—ethical philosophers of Greece, 300—Plato, 301—Aristotle, 302—Epicureans and Stoics, lb.—Epicurus and Zeno, 303—system of Stoics, 304—estimate of the practical philosophy of Greece, 305—Literature of Alexandria, 306—scholastic ethics, 307, 313—Jewish and Mohammedan writers, 308—Augustin's system, 309—doctrines of Aquinas, Scotus, and Brachardine, lb.—William of Ockham and John Gerson, 310—Aquinas, lb.—the Mystics, 311—temptations and errors of the scholastic age, 312—nominalists and realists, lb.—extensive influence of Aquinas's system, 312-18—Spanish writers on scholastic ethics, 314, 322—Grotius's views of Carneades's principles and arguments, 316—Hobbes's principles; see Hobbes—difference between the acts resulting from reason and passion, 331—self-love, 342, 356—difference between it and benevolent affections, 344—definition of moral sentiments, 346—conscience, lb., 402, 407-14—arative, 346—principle of bearing, lb.—origin of benevolence, 347—pity one of its sources, lb.—origin of sense of justice, lb.—erime and duty, lb.—power of association in regard to remorse, 368—moral faculty uniformly spoken of as one, lb.—moral approbation, lb.—right and wrong, duty and virtue, lb.—comparative merits of various religions, 374—ethics should contain principles recognised by every religion, lb.—honour, cowardice, and duelling, 375—social affections and malevolent passions, 386—humility, 381—love of praise, 382—utility and virtue, lb., 383, 401-2, 409—direct object of ethics, 384—intimately connected with law, lb.—Stewart's ethical theory, 393—principal object of conscience to govern our voluntary exertions, lb.—manner as an index of character and disposition, 395—Dr T. Brown on sympathy and conscience, 399—defect of the terms association and suggestion, 401—the laws of God founded on principle of promoting happiness, 427—question why evil exists, lb.—virtue must be loved for its own sake, 403—primary pleasures, pains, and appetites, 405—their number not determined, lb.—two sects, the partisans of the selfish principle, and the advocates of benevolence, 406—secondary desires or pleasures, 406—anger, 407—magnanimity, lb.—operation of conscience in reference to religious systems, 411—ethical philosophy of Scotland, lb.—of Germany; Kant, 412. See Ethics.
Ethnics, IX. 375—Aristotle on, III. 318—practical, XV. 481—of the Hindus, XVII. 428—of the Egyptians, 430. See Ethical Philosophy, Moral Philosophy, Duty, Conscience.

FIGURE AND SIZE OF THE EARTH, IX. 546; VII. 678; X. 393, 404; I. 591—no correct information of, derived from antiquity, lb.—attempt of Brahe to measure an arc of the meridian, lb.—first modern measurement of any accuracy, by Sacchius, 502—measurement of an arc by Norwood, Ferrel, and Picard, lb.; VI. 218—Richer's observation that a pendulum beats slower at the equator, I. 502—explanation of this by Newton and Huygens, lb.—determination of from the earth by Newton, 501—measurements by Cassini and Fontenelle, 509; VI. 218—Maclaurin's

FIL—FIN

view of earth's figure, I. 656—measurement of degrees of latitude under equator and within arctic circle, 657, 8; V. 144; VII. 199—measurement of degree of latitude by La Callee, I. 658—measurement of degrees by Delambre, &c., 659—various estimates of amount of depression at poles, 660—measurement of a perpendicular to the meridian by Broussaud, lb.—estimate of earth's figure by vibrations of pendulum, lb.—Kater's convertible pendulum, lb.—Ivory's estimate of earth's ellipticity, 661—analytical investigations to determine earth's figure, by Clairaut, Legendre, and Laplace, lb.; III. 702—the demonstration completed by Ivory, I. 661—discussions regarding earth's figure led to an examination of the theory of the tides, 662—corrigendum in this article, XXI. 361, n. 2. See Trigonometrical Survey.

creation, VII. 454—final conflagration,

Delagoa, 702—erratic blocks, 703—
scratches on rocks, lb.—Dolomieu on
geology, VIII. 36, 97—knowledge of
it necessary in draining, 136—silt-
ing of seas and rivers, 193—geological re-
lations of insects, IX. 160—earth's
figure proves its former fluidity, 574
—fossil inferiora, XV. 56—elevation
of Norwegian shores, XVI. 263—basis
of geology, XVII. 503—epochs of, lb.—
circumstances affecting the present
order of phenomena, 516—formation
of deltas, XVII. 524; VIII. 507—ap-
plication of physiology to, XVII. 587

large quantities of limestone and
chalk produced by animals, XXI.
1921. See Collyer, Fossil, Physical

ticles generally.
GEOMANCY, X. 421; VIII. 38.
GEOMETRICAL PROPORTION, II. 439.
GEOMETRY, X. 422—history of, lb.—
plane, 422—of solids, 457—the genius
of the Greeks beautifully displayed
in, I. 435, 586-81—contributions to, by
Euclid, Archimedes, and Apollonius,
435—geometrical analysis, lb.—works
of Regiomontanus, lb.—introduction
of trigonometry and decimal fractions,

and Cavalieri, 424—exhaustions
of the ancients, lb.—idea of quantities
infinitely great and small introduced
by Kepler, 438—generation of solids
by means of, lb.—Kepler's view of
the composition of circles, lb.—obser-
vations on lines and areas, lb.—pur-
pose served by the doctrine of quan-
tities infinitely small in size, and in-
finitely great in number, 438—the
cycloid, lb.—results of introduction
of algebra, 444—Descartes' Geometria,
lb.—Fermat's method of drawing tan-
gents to curves, 445—geometrical
and algebraic method of treating
quantity, lb.—high value set on the
geometrical construction of problems

GEO

by the Greeks, I. 585—attempts to as-
certain relation of diameter to the
circumference, lb.—inconmensurability
of the circle, 384—best elementary
works on, lb.—elementary plane
rests on the combined properties of
the straight line and the circle, lb.—in-
struments required in solving the
common problems, 585—best manner
of treating lines and curves of the
second order, lb.—improvement made
in trigonometry during the last cen-
tury, lb.—Descartes' discoveries, 594
—application of algebra to, II. 424,
489—Dr. Boldeus on the nature of
evidence in, IV. 514—characters of ab-
breviations in, VI. 325—intimate con-
nection of dynamics with, VIII. 381
—applications of the fluxional calcu-
lus to, IX. 703—Egyptian knowledge
of, XVII. 456—ancient geometrical anal-
ysis, XX. 359. See Angles, Circle,
Trigonometry, &c.
Geometry (New), state of the sciences
when Newton arose, I. 518—problem
of quadrature of circle solved by him,
lb.—Wallis's Arithmetic of Infinites,
lb.—discovery of Fluxions by Newton,
519; XIV. 318, 321—Newton the first,
and Leibnitz the second, inventor of
the new infinitesimal analysis, I. 520
—controversy relative to the dis-
covery, 521, et seq.—steps by which
the mathematical sciences were pre-
pared for the new analysis, 525—this
analysis first appeared in the method
of exhaustion, lb.—advanced a step
in Cavalieri's method of indivisibles,
lb.—Descartes' application of algebra
to geometry of curves, lb.—method
of Cavalieri improved and extended
by Torricelli, Barrow, &c., lb.—hastily
appeared Newton and Leibnitz, who
made their discoveries separately, 526
—rejection of the higher powers of
the differences of the variable quantities
by Leibnitz, liable to objection, lb.—
the two problems into which the
analysis, thus constituted, is divided,
526-8—manner in which Newton pro-
ceeded, 526—the fluxional and dif-
ferential calculus, two modifications
of one general method, 527—the in-
troduction of the analysis greatly en-
larged the domain of the mathematical
sciences, lb.—problems of mechanics
and mechanics, to which it was extended,
lb.—is peculiarly adapted to physical
researches, 528—problem of integra-
tion, 528-9—calculus first published
by Leibnitz, 530—writers by whom it
was explained and improved, 531-2—
its firm establishment in Europe, 532
—English mathematicians fell behind
the French in the integrations of dif-
ferential or fluxional equations, 533
—opponents of the new method, lb.—
contributions to the science by
Cotes, Taylor, Bradley, MacLaurin,
&c., 576, 598-9—the Italian mathe-
maticians contributed to the advance-
ment of the higher calculus, 599—
Euler's method of determining parti-
cular integrals, lb.—extension of the
modern calculus by that of partial
differences, lb.—application of it by
Euler and D'Alembert, 600—calculus
of variations, lb.—distinction between
differentials and variations, lb.—in-
tegration of differential equations, lb.—
modifications of theory of infinitesimal
calculus, lb.—the method of prime
and ultimate ratios preferred by New-
ton, 601—improved by Robins and
Lauder, lb.—method of derivative
functions, lb.—works on prime and
ultimate ratios, 602. See Calculus,

I. 644—refraction of, 652; V. 725—solar, 726 at different latitudes, VI. 748—freezing point of mercury, 262—induce of heat on action of chemical affinity, 357—capacity of air for, at different degrees of rarity, 750—is only light in a latent form, 754—its propagation through solids, liquids, and gases, 759—performed by tremulous pulses, lb.—cold the diminution of, VII. 57—Wollaston's cryophorus, 664—property of some bodies of becoming electrical by heat, VIII. 571, 595—its influence on the electric fluid in metals, 599—evolution of, by electricity, 626; XXI. 672—its effect on fluidity, XII. 5; and on the motion of fluids, 75—its relations to air, 114—latent heat of aqueous vapour, 129—Leslie on, XIII. 245—refraction of, 332—its relation to light, 364—its influence on magnetism, 699, 695, 716—connection between the heat and magnetism of the earth, 695—its effect on bodily strength, XIV. 429—death from, 699—its effect on the health of bakers, &c., 510—sensation of, 608; XVII. 662—heat of the lunar and stellar rays, XIV. 717—utility of the thermometer in improving the philosophy of, 721—temperature of the earth's surface and interior, XV. 179—heating

HUME (David), XI. 720, 564—his estimate of Machiavelli, I. 24—on modern improvements in monarchical government, ib.—on the statutes of Henry VII., 37-8—on the influence of the civil war of 1619, 48—on cause and effect, 78, 211, et seq. 283; XVIII. 456, 410—his Treatise of Human Nature, I. 206-7, 221, 335—its effects on literature, 208—his division of the objects of knowledge, ib.—admitted the existence of only impressions and ideas, 209, 219—his scepticism, 209, 215—his refutation of attempts to demonstrate self-evident truths, 210—benefits which have resulted from his reasonings, ib.—his distinction between the sensitive and cogitative parts of our nature, 212—on our belief in the permanency of the laws of nature, ib.—on the course of nature, and succession of ideas, ib.—his reformation of the philosophical vocabulary, 213—instance of his sincerity in the search after truth, 216—Butler's opinion of his Essay, 217—virtuous private character of, 352—anticipated in some of his doctrines, 425—considered his Inquiry concerning the Principles of Morals as the best of his writings, 354—merits of that work, 355, et seq.—his style, 354—his last illness, 424—on the state of agriculture under the feudal system, II. 252—on God's government of the universe, III. 176, n.—on balance of power, IV. 316—his philosophy, 477—his injustice to Prymne, 629—on miracles, VI. 622, 627; XV. 293—on the effect of a progressive rise of prices in stimulating productive industry, VII. 160—his character of Oliver Cromwell, 477; of the Marquis of Worcester, XXI. 941—on association of ideas, VIII. 455; XIV. 626—his letter to Gibbon, X. 699; to Dr Reid, XIX. 106—his faults as a historian, XI. 493—Hurd's answer to, 759; XXI. 772—on the origin of ideas, XII. 296—on rates of interest, 319; XVIII. 265; n. 1—on law, XIII. 176, 177—on perception, XIV. 605—on memory, 615—on the idea of power, 633—on belief of testimony, 641—on necessity, 699—on instinctive benevolence, XV. 453—on government, XVI. 235—on Oslan, 666; 662—on political economy, XVIII. 266—his conduct to J. J. Rousseau, XIX. 563—Smell's Life of, XX. 493—Adam Smith's account of his death and character, XX. 417; and Lord Hailes's Latin version of it, VII. 691.—(Joseph), his financial opposition

LEHRITZ (G. W.), XIII. 202; V. 25—on the merits of Descartes, Hobbes, Bacon, and Campanella, I. 26—his objections to Malebranche's principles relative to cause and effect, 78—his veneration for the Roman law, 92—his misapprehension of Locke's doctrine on the origin of knowledge, 113—the school which he founded strongly discriminated from Locke's, 122—effects of his correspondence, 123—coincidence between him and Colwell concerning innate ideas, lb.—doctrines which he laboured to establish, 124—his theory of pre-established harmony, lb., 125—contrast between his speculations and the doctrines of Locke, 126—his scheme of optimism, lb., et seq.—his work entitled Theodicea, 126-7, 129—maintained necessity, but opposed materialism, 128—on the soul, lb.—injurious influence of his doctrine of fatalism, 129—on the principle of a sufficient reason, 139, et seq.; 536-7—his theory of moods, 136, 255—on the law of continuity, 257-8, 537—on the states of rest and motion of bodies, 257—on the existence of the soul after death, 259—his mind, in reasoning upon metaphysical subjects, biased by geometry, lb.—extension of his principle by Helvetius, 135—on the etymological study of languages, 156, 262—his assertion that all proper names were at first appellatives, 260—his genius, 137—his credulity, 133, 251—value of his speculations in advancing knowledge, lb.—estimate of

LEI—LEN

his character by Bailly, 262—great variety of subjects on which he wrote, lb.—his controversy with Dr Clarke, 133—on space and time, 142—small progress of his tenets in France, 186—contrast between the form of his writings and the character of his mind, 337—favourite maxim of, lb.—his ethical principles where to be found, lb.—citations from, on ethics, 337, 422—on disinterested affection, 338—egregious fallacy contained in a statement of, 338-9—his correspondence with Newton on geometry, 520—was the second inventor of the new infinitesimal analysis, lb.; IX. 632—rapid spread of his geometry on the Continent, I. 520—controversy relative to the new calculus, 521; XI. 318, 321—his rejection of the higher powers of the differences of the variable quantities objectionable, I. 526—comparison between him and Newton, 571—his algebraical discoveries, 595—a great promoter of the Berlin Academy of Sciences, II. 71—an algebraic discovery of James Gregory claimed by, 497—hydrostatical fancy of, IV. 395—his classification of books, 631—ineffectiveness of matter denied by, VIII. 359—on the laws of motion, 361, 364, 366—his envious detraction of Sir Isaac Newton, 377—on the measure of active forces, XIV. 353—on music, XV. 663—on the force and velocity of moving bodies, I. 539; XVIII. 779—on the strength of materials, XX. 763.

MATERIALISTS, XIV. 312; I. 57—fallacy of their reasoning exposed, 58.

Mathematicians, ancient, XX. 359.
MATHEMATICS, XIV. 313—ancient works on, preserved in religious establishments during the dark ages, I. 434—language of, must always consist of two parts, 445—Aristotle on, III. 429—use of definitions in, XIII. 444—establishment of principles in, II.—speculative and practical propositions, II.—mathematical principles how distinguished, II.—utility of the study of, in cultivating the power of reasoning, 449—origin of, XVII. 563—indispensable to the natural philosopher, 571. Maurolycus of Messina, III. 288—his contributions to mathematics, I. 436; XIV. 316—distinguished for skill in optics, 563—formed a right judgment of defects of short-sighted and long-sighted eyes, II.—explained why light admitted through a small hole of any shape, and received upon a plane, is always round, and through a large one always of the shape of the aperture, 563—supposed that light acquires colour by refraction in passing through the water-drop of the rainbow, 563—on optics, XIII. 329; XVI. 332.

MEC—MED

MECHANICAL POWERS, XIV. 348—origin of knowledge of, XVII. 553—electromagnetism applied as a, XXI. 687.—SOLUTION, XIV. 348.
MECHANICS, XIV. 349; XVII. 567—small progress made in the science before end of 16th century, I. 474—Guido Ubaldi, II.—Stevinus, II.—revolution effected by Galileo, 475—principle of virtual velocities enumerated, 533—Varignon's Projet d'une Nouvelle Mécanique, 535-6—Newton's Principia, 534—improvements by Leibnitz, II.—force of moving body estimated by Descartes and Leibnitz, 538—controversy which ensued, 539, et seq.—principles relative to force of moving bodies, 540—controversy as to the situation, 541—problems solved in times of Newton and Leibnitz, 542—contributions of Halley, 542-4—skill of the Druids in, VIII. 207—Aristotle's wheel, XIX. 439. —(Sir Isaac), XVI. 175—remarks on some queries of, I. 49—effects of his discoveries, 149—his contributions to metaphysics, ib.; to optics, I. 515, et seq., 631, 472; II. 29; VI. 632; XIII. 331, et seq.; XVI. 361, 403, 427, 429, 434, 446, 510; to astronomy, I. 560-2; III. 743; to mathematics, XIV. 318, 321—state of the sciences when he appeared, I. 518—problem of quadrature of circle ib.—binomial theorem, ib.—his discovery of fluxions, 519, et seq.; IX. 632—his controversy with Leibnitz, I. 529; XIV. 318, 321; VIII. 377—his works on the new analysis, I. 539—his tract

110
INDEX.
NEW—NIC

on the quadrature of curves, I. 331—his book of Fluxions, ib.—discoveries contained in his Principles, 556—character of that work, 139; X. 450; XIV. 319, 352—character of his Optics, II. 99; I. 139—his discovery of gravitation, 354, et seq.; IV. 175—on the causes of the irregularity of the moon's motion, I. 569—determined mean quantity of retrogradation of line of moon's nodes, ib.—on the precession of the equinoxes, 562—his philosophy has received repeated improvements, all confirming its truth, 563—on the tides, 563-4; XXI. 269—on orbits of comets, I. 565; VII. 137—on the nature of gravity, I. 568—compared with Leibnitz, 571—his thermometer, 641; IV. 395; XXI. 237—his theory of the moon, I. 565—his telescope, II. 99; XVI. 360; XXI. 118—his optical errors, II. 160—his theory of sound, 115—on ether, 198; XIV. 532—his formula of the chords of multiple arcs, 493—on the history and chronology of Antiquity, III. 245—his attempt to solve a problem of Apollonius, 289—on comets, IV. 46—on the gradation of the atmospheric density, 538—his burning-mirrors, V. 745—on chemical attraction, VI. 356—Dr Clarke's Latin translation of his Optics, 729—on the collimator, VII. 37—on haloes, 372—on crystallization, 386—his obligations to Dr Barrow, VIII. 377—Leibnitz's curious defraction of, ib.—his inverse problem of centripetal forces, 384—on electricity, 566—on the earth's figure, I. 561; IX. 547; XVII. 498—on projectiles, XI. 43—on hydrodynamics, XII. 3, 57, 76, 82; XIX. 257—his correspondence with Locke, I. 118; XIII. 413—his temper perturbable, ib., n.—his mental aberrations, 413, 416—on magnetism, 687—on the lever, XIV. 369—his reflecting microscope, XV. 41—on the source of planetary motion, 574—on the attraction of mountains, 583—compared with Aristotle, XVII. 483—his rules of philosophizing, 441-2—his definition of fluid, 567—on pneumatics, XVIII. 192, 113, 115, 117, 118—on the resistance to calling bodies, 645—on measure of force, 789—on the resistance of fluids, XIX. 165—his demonstration of the 28th lemma of the first Book of his Principles, XX. 554—on moving forces, 565—on cohesion, 751—on the alternate attraction and repulsion of light by bodies, 752. See Newtonian Philosophy.

PALEY (William), D.D., XVI. 760—on Tucker's writings, I. 114—disputed the existence of a moral faculty, 225—followed the opinions of Bishop Law, 226—on moral sense, 228—one of the brightest ornaments of the English church in the 18th century, 372—did not own his system to Hume, he—fitter for business than philosophy, lb.—his genius and practical bent, lb., 373—his style, lb.—his Evidence of Christianity, Moral and Political Philosophy, and Natural Theology, lb.—on virtue, 374—erroneous views on honour, lb.—occasionally a tax moralist, 375—his political and ecclesiastical views, 376—on charity, VI. 527—price of the copyright of his Moral Philosophy, VII. 515—on divorce, VIII. 72—on the character of reformers, IX. 536, n. 3—on hypocrisy, IX. 142; I. 374—on instinct, 226; XII. 364—on the hand, XVII. 618—on prayer, XVIII. 566—on titles, XXI. 114—on the goodness of God, 209.

VOLTAIC ELECTRICITY, XXI. 664—decomposition by means of, VII. 618-9—reanimation of drowned persons by, VIII. 293—Faraday on, 575—medical use of, 641. See Galvanism. —Pile, XXI. 664; I. 623; VIII. 572.

VOLTAIRE (P. M. A. de), XXI. 760—his estimate of Montaigne, I. 53; of La Rochefoucauld, 54—on innate ideas, 251—clearly understood Berkeley's theory of vision, 165—Madame de Staél's division of his literary life into two epochs, 277—advocated liberty at one time and necessity at another, 278—on the force of a moving body, 316—helped to establish the Newtonian philosophy in France, 577—his character of Bayle, IV. 467; of La Bruyère, V. 518—his commentary on Becaria, IV. 304—his visit to Congress, VII. 217—his History of the Crusades, 487—on the Italian opera, VIII. 157—his improvement of theatrical costume, 165—his dramatic works, lb.—on Dryden, 228—his connexion with Frederick II., X. 228—Gibbon's visit to, 585—on the moral doctrines of Christ, XII. 242—his opinion of Machiavel, XIII. 648—his feelings towards Montesquieu, XV. 439—on happiness, XVIII. 52—poetry of, 166—romances of, XIX. 351.

A blank, aged, cream-colored page, likely an endpaper or flyleaf of a book. The page shows signs of wear, including a small dark brown stain near the top left corner and faint smudges throughout.This image shows a blank, aged, cream-colored page, likely an endpaper or flyleaf from an old book. The paper has a slightly textured appearance with some minor discoloration and faint smudges. A small, dark brown stain is visible near the top left corner, possibly from a binding or a spill. The overall tone is warm and off-white, characteristic of old paper.

The page shows signs of wear, including faint smudges and discoloration, particularly along the left edge where a dark binding strip is visible.This image shows a blank, aged, cream-colored page, likely an endpaper or flyleaf from an old book. The paper has a slightly textured appearance with some minor discoloration and faint smudges, particularly along the left edge where a dark binding strip is visible. There are no markings, text, or illustrations on the page.

Embossed circular seal of the Advocatorum Bibliotheca.An embossed circular seal on a dark brown, textured surface. The seal features a laurel wreath surrounding a central sunburst motif. The text "ADVOCATORUM" is arched across the top, and "BIBLIOTHECA" is arched across the bottom, with small dots separating them.
